label: Uganda 1972 Asian Expulsion (DEMO)
subtitle: Historical case study: Asian Ugandans during Idi Amin regime - 90-day forced expulsion
disclaimer: HISTORICAL DEMONSTRATION ONLY. Based on documented events from August-November 1972 when ~80,000 Asian Ugandans were expelled. This is NOT current guidance for Uganda. Educational resource showing what crisis preparedness looks like under authoritarian expulsion.

levels.label: GREEN
levels.name: Pre-Crisis (1971 early)
levels.description: Amin takes power in coup. Situation tense but business continues. Asian community cautiously optimistic.
levels.officialTrigger: January 1971 coup. Obote overthrown. Initial promises of stability and reconciliation.
levels.observableTrigger: Military visible but not openly hostile. Businesses operating. Some political arrests but not targeting Asian community yet. Expatriates beginning quiet departures.

nodes.label:
Clarify citizenship
immediately
checklist: British passport holders - majority had UK passports but never lived in UK
Ugandan citizenship - some had taken it but legal status unclear under new regime
Indian/Pakistani passports - some retained despite living in Uganda for generations
Stateless risk - some families had no clear citizenship anywhere
Register with appropriate High Commission - UK, India, or Pakistan
Document family's entire immigration history to Uganda

nodes.label:
Document all
assets & property
checklist: Property deeds and titles to all real estate
Business registration documents and licenses
Bank account details - all institutions, all family members
Vehicle registration documents
Insurance policies
Stock certificates and investment records
Safe deposit box inventory
Photograph valuable items for future claims
Make copies - keep one set outside Uganda if possible

nodes.label:
Build foreign
network
checklist: Contact family in UK, Canada, India - establish potential landing zones
Professional contacts in target countries - for job references
Religious community connections - temples, mosques abroad
Business partners in other countries
Update addresses and phone numbers regularly
Share basic family information with trusted foreign contacts
Investigate visa requirements for UK, Canada, India NOW

levels.label: YELLOW
levels.name: Increasing Hostility (1971-1972)
levels.description: Anti-Asian rhetoric increases. Random harassment and arrests. Some businesses seized 'irregularly'.
levels.officialTrigger: Official statements about 'economic saboteurs'. Military checkpoints increase. Asian trade licenses reviewed.
levels.observableTrigger: Soldiers demanding bribes more aggressively. Some shops vandalized. Community members disappear temporarily then return traumatized. BBC reporting concerns.

nodes.label:
Assess barriers
to departure
checklist: Financial: can you afford to abandon everything and restart?
Emotional: accepting that you may never return or recover assets
Family: elderly relatives who cannot travel easily
Business: contracts, employees, obligations
Those who left 'early' were criticized. Those who stayed 'too long' lost everything.
UK began restricting entry even for passport holders. Time mattered.
India/Pakistan accepting returnees but capacity limited

levels.label: ORANGE
levels.name: Expulsion Announced (Aug 4, 1972)
levels.description: Amin announces expulsion: all Asians with British passports must leave in 90 days. Deadline: November 8, 1972.
levels.officialTrigger: August 4, 1972 radio announcement. 'God told me in a dream'. Initial deadline: 90 days. Later extended slightly.
levels.observableTrigger: Panic in community. Long lines at High Commissions. Businesses being marked for takeover. Property buyers disappear. Airport chaos begins.

nodes.label:
Verify your
status urgently
checklist: British passport holders - definitely covered
Ugandan citizens initially exempted - but this changed unpredictably
Indian/Pakistani passport holders - initially unclear, later included
Professional classes targeted first - doctors, lawyers, business owners
Go to High Commission daily for updates - information changed constantly
Assumption of safety was deadly - verify your specific situation
Some Ugandan citizens later stripped of citizenship retroactively

nodes.label:
Final asset
liquidation
checklist: Accept ANY reasonable offer - you have no leverage
Transfer money through hawala - banks are compromised
Convert to portable valuables - gold, jewelry, small items
Some buried money/gold hoping to return - most never recovered
Sign over businesses under duress - legal fiction but prevented worse
Do NOT expect fair value - 10-20 cents on dollar was common
Priority: get family out safely. Everything else is secondary.

levels.label: RED
levels.name: Final Days (Oct-Nov 1972)
levels.description: Deadline approaching. Violence increasing. Thousands still waiting for transport. Military takeover of properties accelerating.
levels.officialTrigger: November deadline imminent. Extensions granted selectively. Threats of military action if people don't leave.
levels.observableTrigger: Entebbe airport processing 700+ people daily. Families camping at airport for days. Confiscations routine. Random arrests. Some disappear permanently.

nodes.label:
Move to airport
area NOW
checklist: Do not wait for 'perfect' moment - go NOW
Women and children first if splitting up
Bring only what you can carry - one suitcase per person
Airport processing taking 12-48 hours - bring food, water, blankets
Airport conditions: no privacy, no sanitation, no safety guarantees
Stay together as family unit - separations often permanent
Soldiers at airport were unpredictable - extremely dangerous
Some families camped near airport for days waiting for flights
Do NOT leave airport once there - re-entry was often denied

nodes.label:
Strip down to
bare essentials
checklist: Passport, tickets, medical records only
One change of clothes per person
Medications for journey
Small amount of cash for bribes
Wedding photos, children's vaccination cards if space
Everything else: abandon it
Inspectors will confiscate: cameras, radios, jewelry, cash, electronics
Cavity searches common - hide nothing, risk is too high
Your life and family's safety - only things that matter now
Some hid valuables in clothing linings - if caught, consequences severe

nodes.label:
Airport survival
protocol
checklist: Comply with all demands immediately - no exceptions
Do not argue, explain, or negotiate - just obey
Bribes: small amounts in local currency ready
Protection money: some soldiers demanded payment to 'protect' luggage
Sexual assault risk high - women stayed in groups when possible
Children: keep close at all times, teach them to be silent and invisible
Document checks happened multiple times - soldier would 'lose' papers and demand bribes
Witnesses report bodies at airport - some who resisted or fought back
If separated from family: go to High Commission area immediately
Once on plane, do not celebrate until airborne - planes have been stopped

levels.label: BLACK
levels.name: Post-Deadline (Nov 8+)
levels.description: Deadline passed. Approximately 7,000 remained - stranded, stateless, or refusing to leave. Conditions deteriorated sharply.
levels.officialTrigger: November 8, 1972 deadline elapsed. Military began house-to-house searches. Detention camps established.
levels.observableTrigger: Shops shuttered. Businesses seized. Asian areas emptied. Those remaining lived in fear of arrest or worse. International pressure mounted but slowly.

nodes.label:
Emergency document
application
checklist: UK issued emergency travel documents - but process slow and dangerous
Required multiple trips to High Commission through hostile military
Stateless individuals had no recourse - truly trapped
Red Cross intervened for some cases
UNHCR began processing but with limited capacity
Some countries (India, Pakistan) provided documents retrospectively
Documentation often 'lost' by officials demanding bribes

nodes.label:
Find refugee
pathway
checklist: Canada announced special intake - several hundred accepted
UK under pressure reopened some cases
Some fled overland to Kenya - illegal but only option
Kenyan camps held some temporarily
UNHCR camps in Europe (Treviso, Italy) took small numbers
Religious organizations sponsored some families
Process could take months in dangerous conditions

nodes.label: Survival mode
checklist: Move frequently - staying in one place too dangerous
Rely on remaining community networks
Some hid with sympathetic African Ugandan friends - enormous risk for hosts
Bribery essential - maintain small cash reserve
Avoid all official contact if possible
No work, no income, depleting savings
Malnutrition became common
Mental health crises widespread
Some committed suicide
Children unable to attend school
Medical care virtually unavailable
This was not sustainable - merely surviving until escape possible
